subject: English
question:
Passage: We shall go on the end, we shall fight in France, we shall fight on the seas and oceans, we shall fight with the growing confidence and strength in the air, we shall defend our island, whatever the cost may be, we shall fight on the beaches, we shall fight on the landing grounds, we shall fight in the fields and in the streets, we shall fight in the hills. We shall never surrender, and even if this island or a large part of it was subjugated and starving, then our empire beyond the seas would carry on the struggle, until the New World steps forth to the rescue and the liberation of the Old.

Q1. On the basis of the passage which of the following statements may be said to be correct?
(a) The speaker is encouraging his men for the conquest of France
(b) The speaker is an aggressive and maniacal war-monger
(c) The speaker is not satisfied with the conquest of the island
(d) The speaker is a patriot urging the defence of his motherland

option.A: The speaker is encouraging his men for the conquest of France
option.B: The speaker is an aggressive and maniacal war-monger
option.C: The speaker is not satisfied with the conquest of the island
option.D: The speaker is a patriot urging the defence of his motherland

explanation: The passage is Winston Churchill's famous wartime speech. The speaker urges fighting on every front to defend 'our island', emphasising never surrendering. This clearly shows the patriotic intent of defending one's homeland, not aggression or conquest.

subject: English
question:
Passage: We shall go on the end, we shall fight in France, we shall fight on the seas and oceans... We shall never surrender, and even if this island or a large part of it was subjugated and starving, then our empire beyond the seas would carry on the struggle, until the New World steps forth to the rescue and the liberation of the Old.

Q2. The speaker in the passage wants to go on fighting because
(a) he is a raving lunatic
(b) he is in a state of utter despair
(c) he expects help from other quarters
(d) he is the leader of a suicide squad

option.B: he is in a state of utter despair
option.C: he expects help from other quarters
option.D: he is the leader of a suicide squad

explanation: The speaker mentions 'until the New World steps forth to the rescue and the liberation of the Old.' This clearly indicates that he expects help from other quarters (i.e., the New World/America), which gives him confidence to continue fighting.

subject: English
question:
Passage: We shall go on the end, we shall fight in France... We shall never surrender...

Q3. Which of the following pair of the phrases helps best to bring out the intention of the speaker?
(a) 'Go on to the end', 'shall never surrender'
(b) 'Growing confidence', 'subjugated and starving'
(c) 'Subjugated and starving', 'fighting and landing around'
(d) 'Fighting in the streets', 'subjugated and starving'

option.A: 'Go on to the end', 'shall never surrender'

explanation: 'Go on to the end' and 'shall never surrender' are the two most central phrases that capture the speaker's unwavering determination and resolve to keep fighting and never give up, which is the core intention of the speech.

subject: English
question:
Passage: We shall go on the end, we shall fight in France... We shall never surrender...

Q4. The passage consists of repetitive patterns in syntax and vocabulary. The effect of this style is that it
(a) reveals the speaker's defects in giving a speech
(b) produces the impression of bad poetry
(c) conveys the speaker's helpless situation
(d) reinforces the speaker's basic intention

option.A: reveals the speaker's defects in giving a speech
option.B: produces the impression of bad poetry

explanation: The repetition of 'we shall fight' is a deliberate rhetorical device (anaphora) used to emphasise and reinforce the speaker's basic intention — the unshakeable resolve to fight and never surrender. Repetition in speeches creates emphasis and conviction.

subject: English
question:
Passage: We shall go on the end, we shall fight in France... We shall never surrender...

Q5. The tone of the speaker is
(a) pleading and urging
(b) inspiring and encouraging
(c) discouraging and gloomy
(d) menacing and bullying
explanation: The speaker's tone is inspiring and encouraging. He is rallying his people with confidence and determination, urging them to fight on every front and never give up. The speech is designed to boost morale and inspire courage, not to plead or bully.

subject: English
question:
Q6. Choose the word most similar in meaning to 'MEDDLE'.
(a) Disregard
(b) Overlook
(c) Interfere
(d) Free
explanation: 'Meddle' means to interfere in or busy oneself unduly with something that is not one's concern. 'Interfere' is the closest synonym among the given options.

subject: English
question:
Q7. Choose the word most similar in meaning to 'ABJURE'.
(a) Renounce
(b) Run off secretly
(c) Abide
(d) Discuss
explanation: 'Abjure' means to solemnly renounce or give up (a belief, cause, or claim). 'Renounce' is the correct synonym meaning to formally declare one's abandonment of a claim, right, or possession.

subject: English
question:
Q8. Choose the word most similar in meaning to 'ESTRANGE'.
(a) Endanger
(b) To become puzzling
(c) Miscalculate
(d) Alienate
explanation: 'Estrange' means to cause someone to be no longer on friendly terms; to make unfriendly or hostile. 'Alienate' is the correct synonym meaning to cause someone to feel isolated or estranged.

subject: English
question:
Q9. Choose the word most nearly opposite in meaning to 'DEROGATORY'.
(a) Conferred
(b) Immediate
(c) Praising
(d) Private
explanation: 'Derogatory' means showing a critical or disrespectful attitude; belittling. Its antonym is 'Praising', which means expressing approval or admiration — the exact opposite of being derogatory.

subject: English
question:
Q10. Choose the word most nearly opposite in meaning to 'WANE'.
(a) Widen
(b) Poor
(c) Swell
(d) Tight
explanation: 'Wane' means to decrease in vigour, power, or extent; to diminish. Its antonym is 'Swell', which means to grow or increase in size, number, or intensity — the opposite of waning.

subject: English
question:
Q11. Find out which part has an error.
The police has (a) / arrested the thief (b) / who broke into my house (c) / last night. (d)
(a) Part a
(b) Part b
(c) Part c
(d) Part d
explanation: The error is in part (a). 'Police' is a collective noun that takes a plural verb. The correct form should be 'The police have' instead of 'The police has'. Collective nouns referring to institutions like police, army, jury take plural verbs in British English.

subject: English
question:
Q12. Find out which part has an error.
The man who (a) / they thought to be (b) / a gentleman turned out (c) / to be a rogue. (d)
(a) Part a
(b) Part b
(c) Part c
(d) Part d
explanation: There is no error in this sentence. All parts are grammatically correct. The sentence correctly uses 'who' as relative pronoun, the reported speech structure 'thought to be', and the correct idiomatic expression 'turned out to be'. Answer is (d) — no error.

subject: English
question:
Q13. Find out which part has an error.
I told him on his face (a) / that he could not hope (b) / to pass the stringent (c) / Medical examination of the Services Selection Board. (d)
(a) Part a
(b) Part b
(c) Part c
(d) Part d

option.A: I told him on his face
option.D: Medical examination of the Services Selection Board

explanation: The error is in part (a). The correct idiom is 'to his face' not 'on his face'. The phrase 'told him to his face' means to say something directly and openly to someone. 'On his face' is incorrect usage.

subject: English
question:
Q14. Find out which part has an error.
Mohan is one of those boys (a) / who has expressed (b) / willingness for joining (c) / the education tour. (d)
(a) Part a
(b) Part b
(c) Part c
(d) Part d

option.A: Mohan is one of those boys

explanation: The error is in part (b). In the construction 'one of those + plural noun + who/that', the verb after 'who' should agree with the plural noun, not with 'one'. So it should be 'who have expressed' (not 'who has expressed'), because 'boys' is plural.

subject: English
question:
Q15. Find out which part has an error.
I may spend (a) / this summer vacations (b) / with one of my friends (c) / in the back waters of Kerala. (d)
(a) Part a
(b) Part b
(c) Part c
(d) Part d

option.D: in the back waters of Kerala

explanation: The error is in part (b). 'Vacations' is a plural noun, so the demonstrative adjective should be 'these' not 'this'. The correct form is 'these summer vacations'. 'This' is used with singular nouns and 'these' with plural nouns.

subject: English
question:
Q16. According to the weather ________ it is going to be cloudy today.
(a) announcement
(b) indication
(c) prediction
(d) forecast
explanation: 'Weather forecast' is the standard, idiomatic expression used for the prediction of upcoming weather conditions. 'Forecast' specifically refers to the prediction of future weather, making it the most appropriate word in this context.

subject: English
question:
Q17. The villagers ________ the murder of their leader by burning the police van.
(a) protested
(b) avenged
(c) mourned
(d) consoled
explanation: 'Avenged' means to take revenge for a wrong. The villagers retaliated against their leader's murder by burning the police van, which is an act of revenge/vengeance. 'Avenged the murder' is the most contextually appropriate phrase here.

subject: English
question:
Q18. While on the routine flight, the aircraft was hit by a missile and ________ into flames.
(a) fired
(b) burst
(c) caught
(d) engulfed
explanation: 'Burst into flames' is the correct idiomatic expression meaning to suddenly catch fire. This is the standard phrase used when something suddenly ignites. 'Burst' collocates with 'into flames' naturally in English.

subject: English
question:
Q19. Hari got the company car for a ________ price as he was the senior most employee in the company.
(a) reduced
(b) discounted
(c) fixed
(d) nominal
explanation: 'Nominal price' means a very small or token price — much less than the actual value. As the senior-most employee, Hari received the car at a very low/token price, making 'nominal' the most appropriate word in context.

subject: English
question:
Q20. The unruly behaviour of the soldiers _________ their commander.
(a) clashed
(b) aggrieved
(c) incensed
(d) impeached
explanation: 'Incensed' means to make someone very angry; to enrage. The unruly behaviour of soldiers would make their commander extremely angry. 'Incensed' is the most contextually precise word. 'Aggrieved' means feeling resentment, which is less intense than being incensed.

subject: General Knowledge
question:
Q21. Who accorded the title 'Mahatma' to MK Gandhi?
(a) Sardar Patel
(b) Nehru
(c) Sarojini Naidu
(d) Rabindranath Tagore
explanation: The title 'Mahatma' (meaning Great Soul) was bestowed upon Mohandas Karamchand Gandhi by Rabindranath Tagore, the Nobel Prize winning poet. Gandhi first used the title publicly in 1915 when he returned from South Africa.

subject: General Knowledge
question:
Q22. The Non-cooperation Movement started in which year?
(a) 1900
(b) 1921
(c) 1940
(d) 1935
explanation: The Non-Cooperation Movement was launched by Mahatma Gandhi in 1920 and formally started in 1921. It was the first mass movement against British rule, calling on Indians to withdraw cooperation from the British government.

subject: General Knowledge
question:
Q23. During the Middle Ages education was confined only to
(a) Kshatriyas
(b) Brahmins
(c) Peasants
(d) Shudras
explanation: During the Middle Ages in India, formal education was largely confined to Brahmins. They were the custodians of knowledge and learning, and the education system under the gurukul tradition primarily served the upper castes, especially Brahmins.

subject: General Knowledge
question:
Q24. Who among the following was responsible for the spread of Buddhism in Sri Lanka?
(a) Ashoka
(b) Mahavira
(c) Parsavanath
(d) Chandra Gupta Maurya
explanation: Emperor Ashoka was responsible for the spread of Buddhism in Sri Lanka. He sent his son Mahinda and daughter Sanghamitra to Sri Lanka to propagate Buddhist teachings. Mahinda converted the Sri Lankan king Devanampiya Tissa to Buddhism.

subject: General Knowledge
question:
Q25. The elements which have low value of ionization potential are strong
(a) oxidising agents
(b) reducing agents
(c) oxidising and reducing agents depending upon the reactants
(d) none of these

option.C: oxidising and reducing agents depending upon the reactants

explanation: Elements with low ionization potential easily lose electrons. When an element easily loses electrons, it is a good reducing agent (it reduces other substances by donating electrons to them). Metals like alkali metals have low ionization potential and are strong reducing agents.

subject: General Knowledge
question:
Q26. Wayne Rooney, the famous footballer, hails from which country?
(a) Italy
(b) Brazil
(c) Argentina
(d) England
explanation: Wayne Rooney is a famous English footballer who played for Manchester United and the England national team. He is one of England's greatest strikers and their all-time leading goal scorer in international football.

subject: General Knowledge
question:
Q27. The official song 'WakaWaka' of FIFA World Cup held in the year 2010 at South Africa was sung by which popular singer?
(a) Madonna
(b) Destiny Child
(c) Shakira
(d) Rihanna
explanation: 'Waka Waka (This Time for Africa)' was the official song of the 2010 FIFA World Cup held in South Africa. It was performed by Colombian singer Shakira. The song became one of the best-selling World Cup songs of all time.

subject: General Knowledge
question:
Q28. In the year 2011, the ace badminton player Saina Nehwal was honored with which sports award?
(a) Dronacharya award
(b) Rajiv Gandhi Khel Ratna award
(c) Arjun award
(d) None of these
explanation: Saina Nehwal was awarded the Rajiv Gandhi Khel Ratna award (now renamed Major Dhyan Chand Khel Ratna) in 2009-10. In 2011, she received the Padma Bhushan. The Khel Ratna is India's highest sports honour.

subject: General Knowledge
question:
Q29. Term 'Visible Horizon' in astronomy is defined as
(a) The circle of contact of the earth and the cone of visual rays passing through the meridian of the place
(b) The circle of contact of the earth and the cone of visual rays not passing through the meridian of the place
(c) The circle of contact of the earth and the cone of visual rays passing through the point of observation
(d) The circle of contact of the earth and the cone of visual rays not passing through the point of observation

option.A: The circle of contact of the earth and the cone of visual rays passing through the meridian of the place
option.B: The circle of contact of the earth and the cone of visual rays not passing through the meridian of the place
option.C: The circle of contact of the earth and the cone of visual rays passing through the point of observation
option.D: The circle of contact of the earth and the cone of visual rays not passing through the point of observation

explanation: The 'Visible Horizon' is defined as the circle of contact of the earth and the cone of visual rays passing through the point of observation. It is the boundary line where the earth's surface appears to meet the sky as seen from the observer's position.

subject: General Knowledge
question:
Q30. NREGA stands for
(a) National Revenue Engagement Guarantee Association
(b) National Revenue Employment Guarantee Act
(c) National Rural Employment Guarantee Association
(d) National Rural Employment Guarantee Act
explanation: NREGA stands for National Rural Employment Guarantee Act, enacted in 2005. It guarantees 100 days of employment per year to adult members of rural households willing to do unskilled manual work. It was later renamed MGNREGA.

subject: General Knowledge
question:
Q31. River Sutlej originates from
(a) Northern slopes of the Kailash range
(b) Spring at Verinarg
(c) Rakas Lake
(d) Amarkantak plateau

option.A: Northern slopes of the Kailash range

explanation: The Sutlej River originates from Rakas Lake (Rakshastal) near Mount Kailash in Tibet. It flows westward through the Himalayan ranges into India and is one of the five rivers of Punjab. Spring at Verinarg is the source of River Jhelum.

subject: General Knowledge
question:
Q32. Limonitic ore is the ore of which metal?
(a) Iron
(b) Aluminium
(c) Zinc
(d) Cobalt
explanation: Limonite is an iron ore — a mixture of hydrated iron(III) oxide-hydroxides. It is an important ore of iron and gives a yellowish-brown colour. Other ores of iron include hematite (Fe₂O₃) and magnetite (Fe₃O₄).

subject: General Knowledge
question:
Q33. Black revolution relates to which of the following?
(a) Self-dependence in foodgrains production
(b) Self-dependence in milk production
(c) Self-dependence in petroleum/crude oil
(d) None of these
explanation: Black Revolution is associated with self-dependence in petroleum/crude oil production. The colour 'black' refers to crude oil/petroleum. Green Revolution = foodgrains, White Revolution = milk, Blue Revolution = fisheries.

subject: General Knowledge
question:
Q34. Karrar is the unmanned bomber aircraft of
(a) Iraq
(b) Iran
(c) Turkey
(d) Pakistan
explanation: Karrar (meaning 'striker' in Persian) is an Iranian long-range jet-powered unmanned combat aerial vehicle (UCAV). It was unveiled by Iran in 2010 and is capable of carrying bombs and air-to-air missiles.

subject: General Knowledge
question:
Q35. Who is the author of book 'Keeping the Faith: Members of a Parliamentarian'?
(a) David Omand
(b) Raja Shehadeh
(c) Raghav Bhal
(d) Somnath Chatterjee
explanation: 'Keeping the Faith: Memoirs of a Parliamentarian' is a book written by Somnath Chatterjee, a veteran CPI(M) politician who served as the Speaker of the Lok Sabha from 2004 to 2009.

subject: General Knowledge
question:
Q36. ________ is a thyroid hormone which controls the balance of calcium in the body.
(a) Calcitonin
(b) Thyroxine
(c) Calmodulin
(d) All of these
explanation: Calcitonin is a hormone produced by the thyroid gland (parafollicular C cells) that regulates calcium levels in the blood. It lowers blood calcium levels by inhibiting bone breakdown (osteoclast activity) and reducing calcium absorption in the intestines.

subject: General Knowledge
question:
Q37. The cell wall in plants is interrupted by narrow pores carrying fine strands of cytoplasm which interlink the contents of the cells. These strands are called:
(a) Plasmohole
(b) Microvilli
(c) Plasmodesmata
(d) Plasmalemma
explanation: Plasmodesmata (singular: plasmodesma) are microscopic channels which traverse the cell walls of plant cells and some algal cells, enabling transport and communication between them. They are cytoplasmic strands that link adjacent plant cells.

subject: General Knowledge
question:
Q38. Who is the cricketer bestowed with an honorary commission in the Territorial Army?
(a) Sachin Tendulkar
(b) Kapil Dev
(c) Virendra Sehwag
(d) Harbhajan Singh
explanation: Sachin Tendulkar was conferred with the honorary rank of Group Captain in the Indian Air Force in 2010, making him the first sportsperson to receive this honour. He was also honoured by the Territorial Army with an honorary commission.

subject: General Knowledge
question:
Q39. The transport phenomenon occurs only in ________ state of a gas and is ________.
(a) non-equilibrium, irreversible
(b) non-equilibrium, reversible
(c) equilibrium, irreversible
(d) equilibrium, reversible
explanation: Transport phenomena (diffusion, viscosity, thermal conduction) in gases occur only in the non-equilibrium state — when there are gradients in properties like temperature, pressure, or concentration. These processes are also irreversible thermodynamically as they increase entropy.

subject: General Knowledge
question:
Q40. Which of the following compounds form nitrites with nitrous acid?
(a) Primary amines
(b) Secondary amines
(c) Tertiary amines
(d) All of these
explanation: Secondary amines react with nitrous acid (HNO₂) to form N-nitrosamines (nitrites/nitrosamines). Primary amines form diazonium salts, and tertiary amines form nitrosamine salts. Only secondary amines form stable nitrosamines with nitrous acid.

subject: Mathematics
question:
Q41. The number whose square is equal to the difference of the squares of 37 and 23 is
(a) 45.09
(b) 28.98
(c) 47.09
(d) 28

subject: Mathematics
question:
Q42. The average age of 8 men is increased by 4 years when one of them whose age is 30 years is replaced by a new man. What is the age of new man?
(a) 55 years
(b) 62 years
(c) 42 years
(d) 69 years
explanation: When average increases by 4 for 8 men, total increase = 8 × 4 = 32 years. New man's age = 30 + 32 = 62 years.

subject: Mathematics
question:
Q43. A vessel, full of water, weighs 24 kg. When the vessel is 1/4 full, it weighs 9 kg. Find the weight of empty vessel.
(a) 4 kg
(b) 5 kg
(c) 8 kg
(d) 3 kg
explanation: Let vessel weight = V and full water weight = W. V + W = 24 and V + W/4 = 9. Subtracting: 3W/4 = 15, so W = 20 kg. V = 24 - 20 = 4 kg.

subject: Mathematics
question:
Q44. If a – b = 4 and ab = 45, find the value of a³ – b³.
(a) 604
(b) 370
(c) 253
(d) 199

subject: Mathematics
question:
Q47. A man's average monthly expenditure for the first four months of the year was Rs. 231.25. For the next five months, the average monthly expenditure was Rs. 22.75 more than what it was during the first four months. If the person spent Rs. 605 in all during the remaining three months of the year, find what percentage of his annual income of Rs. 3500 did he save in the year?
(a) 10%
(b) 15%
(c) 20%
(d) 25%
explanation: First 4 months expenditure = 4 × 231.25 = Rs. 925. Next 5 months avg = 231.25 + 22.75 = Rs. 254. Expenditure = 5 × 254 = Rs. 1270. Last 3 months = Rs. 605. Total expenditure = 925 + 1270 + 605 = Rs. 2800. Savings = 3500 – 2800 = Rs. 700. % savings = 700/3500 × 100 = 20%.

subject: Mathematics
question:
Q48. The average age of students of a class is 15.8 years. The average age of boys in the class is 16.4 years and that of the girls is 15.4 years. The ratio of the number of boys to the number of girls in the class is
(a) 1:2
(b) 2:3
(c) 3:4
(d) 3:5
explanation: Using weighted average (allegation method): Boys avg = 16.4, Girls avg = 15.4, Overall = 15.8. Difference: Boys from overall = 16.4–15.8 = 0.6; Girls from overall = 15.8–15.4 = 0.4. Ratio Boys:Girls = 0.4:0.6 = 2:3. Wait — recalculating: Boys deviation = 0.6, Girls deviation = 0.4. So ratio = 0.4:0.6 = 2:3. Answer is (b) 2:3.

subject: Mathematics
question:
Q49. 18 buckets of water fill a tank when the capacity of each bucket is 8 litres. How many buckets will be needed to fill the same tank, if the capacity of the bucket is 12 litres?
(a) 12
(b) 13.5
(c) 24
(d) cannot be determined due to insufficient data

option.D: cannot be determined due to insufficient data

explanation: Total capacity of tank = 18 × 8 = 144 litres. Number of buckets with 12 litre capacity = 144 / 12 = 12 buckets.

subject: Mathematics
question:
Q50. Srinivasan invests two equal amounts in two banks giving 8% and 12% rate of interest respectively. At the end of year the interest earned is Rs. 1500. Find the sum invested in each.
(a) Rs. 8500
(b) Rs. 15000
(c) Rs. 7500
(d) Rs. 17000
explanation: Let each amount = P. Interest = P×8/100 + P×12/100 = P×20/100 = P/5 = 1500. So P = Rs. 7500.

subject: Mathematics
question:
Q51. The simple interest accrued on a sum of money at the end of four years is 1/5th of its principal. What is the rate of interest per annum?
(a) 4%
(b) 5%
(c) 6%
(d) Inadequate data

subject: Mathematics
question:
Q52. A sum of Rs. 2600 is lent out into two parts, one at 9% and another at 7%. If the total annual income is Rs. 206, find the money lent at 7%.
(a) Rs. 1400
(b) Rs. 900
(c) Rs. 1600
(d) Rs. 1200

subject: Mathematics
question:
Q53. In an election between two candidates, 70% of the voters cast their votes, out of which 2% of the votes were declared invalid. A candidate got 7203 votes which was 60% of the total valid votes. Find the total number of voters enrolled in that election.
(a) 18050
(b) 17500
(c) 17000
(d) 7203
explanation: Valid votes = 7203 / 0.6 = 12005. Total votes cast = 12005 / 0.98 = 12250 (approx). Total voters = 12250 / 0.7 = 17500.

subject: Mathematics
question:
Q54. The price of Kerosene was increased by 11%. What percent a house holder must reduce his consumption of kerosene so as not to increase his expenditure?
(a) 11%
(b) 9.9%
(c) 11.09%
(d) 8.25%
explanation: Required reduction % = [increase% / (100 + increase%)] × 100 = [11/111] × 100 ≈ 9.9%.

subject: Mathematics
question:
Q55. 75 pupils from a school appeared for an examination and 80% of them passed. Another school entered 10 more pupils than the first school and five pupils less than the first school passed. The pass % of in the second school was
(a) 75%
(b) 84%
(c) 72%
(d) 64.7%
explanation: First school: 75 pupils, 80% passed = 60 passed. Second school: 75+10 = 85 pupils appeared. Passed = 60–5 = 55. Pass% = 55/85 × 100 ≈ 64.7%.

subject: Mathematics
question:
Q56. Divide Rs. 80 in the proportion of 3:6:7.
(a) Rs. 10, Rs. 35, Rs. 40
(b) Rs. 15, Rs. 30, Rs. 35
(c) Rs. 15, Rs. 35, Rs. 30
(d) Rs. 10, Rs. 40, Rs. 35
explanation: Total parts = 3+6+7 = 16. Each part = 80/16 = 5. First share = 3×5 = Rs.15, Second = 6×5 = Rs.30, Third = 7×5 = Rs.35.

subject: Mathematics
question:
Q57. Calculate the amount on Rs. 1875 for 2 years at 4% per annum, compounded yearly.
(a) Rs. 676
(b) Rs. 776
(c) Rs. 1778
(d) Rs. 2028

subject: Mathematics
question:
Q58. If x varies as y and x = 8 when y = 15, then the value of x when y = 10 is
(a) 5
(b) 15/8
(c) 8/15
(d) 16/3

subject: Mathematics
question:
Q59. In a mixture of 60 litres, the ratio of milk and water is 2:1. What amount of water must be added to make the ratio of milk and water as 1:2?
(a) 42 Litres
(b) 56 Litres
(c) 60 Litres
(d) 77 Litres
explanation: Milk = 40L, Water = 20L. New ratio 1:2 means water = 2×milk = 2×40 = 80L. Water to add = 80–20 = 60 litres.

subject: Mathematics
question:
Q60. A sum of Rs. 10,000 is lent partly at 6% and the remaining at 10% p.a. If the yearly interest on the average is 9.2%, the money lent at 10% is
(a) Rs. 8000
(b) Rs. 8500
(c) Rs. 5000
(d) Rs. 2000

subject: Reasoning
question:
Q61. Choose the word which is least like the other words in the group.
(a) Garo
(b) Khasi
(c) Kangra
(d) Jayantia
explanation: Garo, Khasi, and Jayantia are all tribes/hill communities of the Meghalaya region in Northeast India. Kangra is a district in Himachal Pradesh and is not a tribal group from Northeast India, making it the odd one out.

subject: Reasoning
question:
Q62. Choose the word which is least like the other words in the group.
(a) Virgo
(b) Pisces
(c) Cancer
(d) Orion
explanation: Virgo, Pisces, and Cancer are all zodiac signs (signs of the horoscope/astrological signs). Orion is a constellation but is not one of the 12 zodiac signs. Hence, Orion is the odd one out.

subject: Reasoning
question:
Q63. Choose the word which is least like the other words in the group.
(a) Shoulder
(b) Foot
(c) Finger
(d) Elbow
explanation: Shoulder, Finger, and Elbow are all joints or parts of the upper body/arm. Foot belongs to the lower limb/leg. Hence, Foot is the odd one out as the others are all related to the arm/upper body.

subject: Reasoning
question:
Q64. Choose the word which is least like the other words in the group.
(a) Canoe
(b) Dingy
(c) Yacht
(d) Igloo
explanation: Canoe, Dingy (Dinghy), and Yacht are all types of small watercraft/boats. An Igloo is a dome-shaped dwelling made of compacted snow, built by Inuit people. Hence, Igloo is the odd one out.

subject: Reasoning
question:
Q65. Choose the word which is least like the other words in the group.
(a) Inn
(b) Club
(c) Motel
(d) Hostel
explanation: Inn, Motel, and Hostel are all types of accommodation/lodging establishments where travellers can stay. A Club is a social or recreational organisation, not an accommodation establishment. Hence, Club is the odd one out.

subject: Reasoning
question:
Q66. Liquor: Drink
(a) Bread: Butter
(b) Tea: Beverage
(c) Sniff: Inhale
(d) Water: Sip
explanation: Liquor is a type of Drink (specific to general relationship). Similarly, Tea is a type of Beverage. The relationship is that of a specific example to its broader category. Tea : Beverage follows the same specific-to-general pattern.

subject: Reasoning
question:
Q67. Evaporation: Cloud
(a) Mountain: Snow
(b) Book: Pages
(c) Pressure: Atmosphere
(d) Tension: Breakdown
explanation: Evaporation leads to the formation of a Cloud (cause and effect). Similarly, Tension leads to Breakdown (cause and effect). Both pairs represent a process that leads to a resultant state or event.

subject: Reasoning
question:
Q68. Barrel: Vial
(a) Book: Pamphlet
(b) Book: Reader
(c) Brochure: Compiler
(d) Length: Height
explanation: A Barrel is a large container and a Vial is a very small container — the relationship is large to small version of the same type of thing. Similarly, a Book is a large publication and a Pamphlet is a small publication. Both pairs represent large vs. small versions of the same category.

subject: Reasoning
question:
Q69. Cream: Cosmetics
(a) Tiger: Forest
(b) Mountain: Valley
(c) Magazine: Editor
(d) Teak: Wood
explanation: Cream is a type of Cosmetic (specific to category). Similarly, Teak is a type of Wood (specific to category). Both pairs follow the specific-to-general category relationship.

subject: Reasoning
question:
Q70. Carnivore: Herbivore
(a) Flesh: Plant
(b) Horse: Lion
(c) Camel: Giraffe
(d) Animal: Bird
explanation: Carnivore eats flesh while Herbivore eats plants — they are opposites in diet. Lion is a carnivore and Horse is an herbivore — they represent examples of carnivore and herbivore respectively, following the same contrasting relationship.

subject: Reasoning
question:
Q71. Hamlet: Macbeth: Faustus — What do these three have in common?
(a) They are princes
(b) They are plays by Shakespeare
(c) They are characters from various dramas
(d) They were romantic heroes

option.C: They are characters from various dramas

explanation: Hamlet and Macbeth are plays/characters by Shakespeare, while Faustus is a character from Marlowe's 'Doctor Faustus'. All three are famous characters from various dramas. They are not all by Shakespeare (Faustus is by Marlowe), so option (c) is the most accurate.

subject: Reasoning
question:
Q72. Vesuvius: Etna: Kilimanjaro — What do these three have in common?
(a) These are sites of volcanoes
(b) These are island countries
(c) These are hills of Italy
(d) These lie in polar region
explanation: Vesuvius (Italy), Etna (Sicily, Italy), and Kilimanjaro (Tanzania) are all famous volcanic mountains. Vesuvius and Etna are active volcanoes. Kilimanjaro is a dormant stratovolcano. All three are sites of volcanoes.

subject: Reasoning
question:
Q73. Knot: Watt: Fathom — What do these three have in common?
(a) The terms are used by sailors
(b) The terms are used for installing electricity
(c) The terms are connected with rope
(d) They are units of measurement

option.A: The terms are used by sailors
option.B: The terms are used for installing electricity
option.C: The terms are connected with rope

explanation: Knot (unit of speed for ships/aircraft), Watt (unit of power), and Fathom (unit of depth in water = 6 feet) are all units of measurement. They measure different things but all are measurement units.

subject: Reasoning
question:
Q74. Barauni: Digboi: Ankleshwar — What do these three have in common?
(a) They are famous for oil fields
(b) They are famous for religious places
(c) They are tourist places of South India
(d) They are famous for handlooms

option.A: They are famous for oil fields
option.B: They are famous for religious places
option.C: They are tourist places of South India

explanation: Barauni (Bihar), Digboi (Assam), and Ankleshwar (Gujarat) are all famous for oil fields and petroleum refineries. Digboi has India's oldest oil well. Barauni and Ankleshwar also have significant oil/petroleum industries.

subject: Reasoning
question:
Q75. Knight: Rook: Bishop — What do these three have in common?
(a) These are missionaries
(b) They are churchmen
(c) These are chessmen
(d) These are ranks of military
explanation: Knight, Rook, and Bishop are all pieces (chessmen) used in the game of chess. While they may have other meanings, in this context they all refer to chess pieces.

subject: Reasoning
question:
Q76. Abhi Bhattacharya: Utpal Dutt: Satyajit Ray — What do these three have in common?
(a) They are character actors
(b) They are directors of Bengali movies
(c) They are famous poets and writers
(d) These personalities belong to Bengal

option.B: They are directors of Bengali movies
option.C: They are famous poets and writers

explanation: Abhi Bhattacharya (actor), Utpal Dutt (actor/director/playwright), and Satyajit Ray (film director) are all famous personalities who belong to Bengal. While Satyajit Ray was a director, not all three were directors, and not all were character actors — the most common link is that all belong to Bengal.

subject: Reasoning
question:
Q77. Voodoo: Sorcery: Necromancy — What do these three have in common?
(a) They are ancient arts found in sculptures
(b) They are terms connected with black magic
(c) They are ancient scripts
(d) They are means of communication of pre-historic age

option.A: They are ancient arts found in sculptures
option.B: They are terms connected with black magic
option.D: They are means of communication of pre-historic age

explanation: Voodoo (African/Caribbean supernatural practice), Sorcery (use of magic powers), and Necromancy (communication with the dead through magic) are all terms associated with black magic or occult practices.

subject: Reasoning
question:
Q78. Rourkela: Bokaro: Durgapur — What do these three have in common?
(a) They are steel plants
(b) They have coal mines
(c) They have atomic plants
(d) They are on the sea coast

option.D: They are on the sea coast

explanation: Rourkela (Odisha), Bokaro (Jharkhand), and Durgapur (West Bengal) are all famous for their integrated steel plants (public sector undertakings). These are major steel manufacturing cities in India.

subject: Reasoning
question:
Q79. Spinach: Fenugreek: Celery — What do these three have in common?
(a) These are cactus plants
(b) These are wild plants
(c) These are wild flowers
(d) These are leafy vegetables
explanation: Spinach, Fenugreek (methi), and Celery are all leafy vegetables — edible plants whose leaves are consumed as food. They are cultivated vegetables, not wild plants or cactus.

subject: Reasoning
question:
Q80. Yeats: Ghalib: Kabir — What do these three have in common?
(a) They were social reformers
(b) They were famous poets
(c) They were saints
(d) They were yoga instructors
explanation: W.B. Yeats (Irish poet), Mirza Ghalib (Urdu/Persian poet), and Kabir (Hindi/Bhojpuri mystic poet) are all famous poets. Though Kabir and Ghalib were also spiritual figures, all three are primarily celebrated as poets.

subject: Reasoning
question:

(a) a — ↑↓ with +∧
(b) b — ∧ with +
(c) c — ∨ with ↑
(d) d — ∧ with +
explanation: The series shows a pattern where two symbols appear in a box, alternating and rotating. Based on the established series pattern in figures 1–5, the next figure should contain ↑↓ combined with +∧, corresponding to answer (a).

explanation: The series shows circles with arrows that rotate 90° clockwise in each step. Following the pattern through figures 1 to 5, the next arrow direction corresponds to answer figure (c) — a downward-curved arrow.

(a) a
(b) b
(c) c

explanation: The problem figures show a pattern where the shape in the box changes with the dot/symbol position rotating. Following the systematic change pattern established by figures 1 to 5, the answer is figure (c).

(b) b

question:
Q86. A cluster of geometrical figures has a dot placed inside. Select the figure from (a), (b), (c), (d) which satisfies the same conditions of placement of the dot as in figure (X). (X shows a dot inside a circle overlapping with a square — dot is in the region common to both circle and square.)
(a) a
(b) b
(c) c
(d) d
explanation: In figure (X), the dot is placed in the region that is inside both the circle and the square (the overlapping region). Answer figure (b) similarly has the dot placed in the region common to both geometric shapes shown.

subject: Reasoning
question:
Q87. A cluster of geometrical figures (circle inside a square with a triangle) has a dot placed inside. Select the figure from (a), (b), (c), (d) which satisfies the same conditions of placement of the dot as in figure (X). (X shows a dot in the region common to both the circle and square but outside the triangle.)
(a) a
(b) b
(c) c
(d) d
explanation: In figure (X), the dot is inside both the circle and the square but outside the triangle. Figure (d) among the answer choices replicates this dot placement condition — inside two overlapping shapes but outside the third.

subject: Reasoning
question:
Q88. A cluster of geometrical figures (rectangle, circle, triangle with two dots) has dots placed inside. Select the figure from (a), (b), (c), (d) which satisfies the same conditions of placement of the dots as in figure (X). (X shows two dots — one inside circle only, another inside both rectangle and circle but outside triangle.)
(a) a
(b) b
(c) c
(d) d
explanation: Figure (X) has two dots with specific region conditions. One dot is inside only the circle (not the rectangle or triangle), and the other is in the intersection of circle and rectangle but outside triangle. Answer (a) replicates both these conditions.

subject: Reasoning
question:
Q89. A cluster of geometrical figures (square, circle, triangle with two dots) has dots placed inside. Select the figure from (a), (b), (c), (d) which satisfies the same conditions of placement of the dots as in figure (X). (X shows a configuration with overlapping shapes and two dots in specific regions.)
(a) a
(b) b
(c) c
(d) d
explanation: In figure (X), the two dots are placed in specific overlapping regions of the circle, triangle, and square. Analysing the dot positions — one inside all three shapes and one inside two specific shapes — answer figure (c) satisfies the same placement conditions.

subject: Reasoning
question:
Q90. A cluster of geometrical figures (ellipse with intersecting lines) has a dot placed inside. Select the figure from (a), (b), (c), (d) which satisfies the same conditions of placement of the dot as in figure (X). (X shows an ellipse with overlapping rectangles and a dot in a specific intersection region.)
(a) a
(b) b
(c) c
(d) d
explanation: In figure (X), the dot is placed in a specific region formed by the intersection of the ellipse and the overlapping geometric shapes. Answer figure (c) replicates the same dot placement condition with analogous overlapping regions.

subject: Reasoning
question:
Q91. A figure (X) is given as the original figure. The original figure is embedded or hidden in one of the answer figures (a, b, c, d). (X shows two diagonal lines going downward-left, like a V-shape rotated.)
(a) a — multiple triangles
(b) b — multiple triangles
(c) c — triangles with horizontal lines
(d) d — triangles with diagonal sections
explanation: Figure (X) shows two diagonal lines forming a downward-pointing V shape. By examining the answer figures, figure (b) contains this pattern hidden within its larger triangle structure — the two diagonal lines of (X) can be clearly traced in the answer figure (b).

subject: Reasoning
question:
Q92. A figure (X) is given as the original figure. The original figure is embedded or hidden in one of the answer figures (a, b, c, d). (X shows a square with two diagonal peak-like shapes inside.)
(a) a — complex diamond shapes
(b) b — inverted shape
(c) c — layered diamond shapes
(d) d — stick figure/costume shape
explanation: Figure (X) shows a square frame with two mountain/peak-shaped lines inside. In answer figure (c), which shows layered diamond shapes, the original figure (X) is hidden — the square with the two peaks can be found embedded in the structure of (c).

subject: Reasoning
question:
Q93. A figure (X) is given as the original figure. The original figure is embedded or hidden in one of the answer figures (a, b, c, d). (X shows a rectangle with a diagonal line inside — like a tilted rod/pencil shape.)
(a) a — two oval shapes overlapping
(b) b — multiple crossing lines
(c) c — diamond with diagonal lines
(d) d — grid pattern
explanation: Figure (X) is a rectangle with a single diagonal line (like a pencil/rod shape). In answer figure (a), which shows two oval/elongated shapes, the original rectangle with diagonal line of figure (X) is embedded within the overlapping structure.

subject: Reasoning
question:
Q94. A figure (X) is given as the original figure. The original figure is embedded or hidden in one of the answer figures (a, b, c, d). (X shows a square with two crossing diagonal lines forming an X shape.)
(a) a — circle with single vertical oval inside
(b) b — circle with double oval inside
(c) c — circle with multiple ovals inside
(d) d — circle with crossing ovals inside

option.A: Circle with single vertical oval inside (a)

explanation: Figure (X) shows two diagonal lines crossing in a square (X pattern). In answer figure (d), which has a circle with crossing oval bands inside, the X-crossing pattern of figure (X) is embedded and can be traced within the structure.

subject: Reasoning
question:
Q95. A figure (X) is given as the original figure. The original figure is embedded or hidden in one of the answer figures (a, b, c, d). (X shows a square with an M-shape inside at the top.)
(a) a — figure with triangular top portions
(b) b — figure with shaded inverted triangle
(c) c — figure with crossing diagonal lines
(d) d — figure with M and external extensions

option.D: Figure with M and external extensions (d)

explanation: Figure (X) is a square containing an M-shape at the top. In answer figure (d), the M-shaped structure inside the square frame is clearly identifiable — the original figure (X) is embedded within the more complex structure of answer (d).

subject: Reasoning
question:
Q96. A part of the figure is missing. Find out from the given option (a), (b), (c) or (d) the right figure to fit in the missing part (X). (The overall figure shows a square divided by X-shaped diagonals with some sections shaded; top-right corner is missing.)
(a) a — plain triangle portion
(b) b — triangle with shading on one side
(c) c — triangle with different shading
(d) d — triangle with shading pattern

option.B: Triangle with shading on one side (b)

explanation: Analysing the overall pattern of the figure with X-shaped diagonals dividing the square into triangular sections, with specific shading in certain sections, the missing top-right corner must match the shading pattern of adjacent sections. Answer (b) correctly completes the pattern.

subject: Reasoning
question:
Q97. A part of the figure is missing. Find out from the given option (a), (b), (c) or (d) the right figure to fit in the missing part (X). (The overall figure shows a large square with a starburst/branch pattern; the bottom-left corner is missing.)
(a) a — branch pattern going lower-left
(b) b — branch pattern going lower-right
(c) c — branch pattern going upper-right
(d) d — branch pattern going upper-left
explanation: The central starburst/branch pattern radiates outward symmetrically. The missing bottom-left corner should contain branches that radiate from the centre toward the lower-left area of the figure. Based on symmetry, answer figure (c) provides the correct branch orientation to complete the pattern.

subject: Reasoning
question:
Q98. A part of the figure is missing. Find out from the given option (a), (b), (c) or (d) the right figure to fit in the missing part (X). (The overall figure shows a large complex figure of overlapping triangles and diagonals; top-right is missing.)
(a) a — matching diagonal section
(b) b — matching section with different shading
(c) c — matching section
(d) d — matching section with different pattern
explanation: The large figure shows an arrangement of triangles and diagonal lines with specific shading pattern. The missing top-right section must seamlessly integrate with the existing diagonal and triangular lines. Answer (b) provides the correct matching section with appropriate shading to complete the overall pattern symmetrically.

subject: Reasoning
question:
Q99. A part of the figure is missing. Find out from the given option (a), (b), (c) or (d) the right figure to fit in the missing part (X). (The overall figure shows a rectangle divided into 6 sections with small circles placed in specific positions; top-right section is missing.)
(a) a — two circles at bottom
(b) b — two circles at different positions
(c) c — one circle
(d) d — one circle at different position
explanation: The rectangle is divided into sections where each section has a specific number and position of circles following a systematic pattern. The missing section (top-right) should have one circle positioned at a specific location to continue the pattern. Answer (d) correctly places the circle to complete the overall arrangement.

subject: Reasoning
question:
Q100. A part of the figure is missing. Find out from the given option (a), (b), (c) or (d) the right figure to fit in the missing part (X). (The overall figure shows a diamond/kite shape divided into sections with X-markings inside; top-right triangular section is missing.)
(a) a — plain right triangle
(b) b — right triangle with small triangle corner
(c) c — right triangle with X marking
(d) d — large right triangle with X marking

option.B: Right triangle with small triangle corner (b)
option.D: Large right triangle with X marking (d)

explanation: The kite/diamond figure has sections with specific X-markings (crossed lines). The missing triangular section at the top-right must continue the X-marking pattern seen in adjacent sections. Answer (c) shows a right triangle with an X-marking that correctly completes the pattern of the overall figure.
